text: We have no disclosures.
In this technical video, we present robotic ventral mesh rectopexy.
Robotic surgery for pelvic organ prolapse repair has been increasingly used in recent years.

The technical advantages of the robot are three-dimensional magnification, articulating instruments with seven degrees of freedom, excellent ergonomics, motion scaling, tremor-free movements, and enhanced intracorporeal steering capability.
In our institution, since two thousand eight, all one hundred robotic mesh rectopexies have been performed.
We use a modified dehaut rectopexy and levotoropexy with slight modifications.
The surgeon secures the mesh to the pelvic floor muscles on either side of the upper anal canal, ensuring sutures are placed without penetrating the rectal wall.
Patient of this video was a sixty-five-year-old female with five years history of obstructive defecation symptoms.
Digital rectal examination showed large rectocele, defecography and dynamic MRI confirmed that patient had a rectocele and also enterocele.

The patient is positioned in a steep Trendelenburg position, which involves tilting the operating table to elevate the pelvis and lower the head, facilitating access to the pelvic region for the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon employs a forearms approach for robotic assistance during the rectopexy procedure, utilizing robotic arms to enhance precision and control in the surgical field.
The surgeon places a 10mm conventional laparoscopic trocar at the umbilicus, followed by three 8mm robotic trocars, and a final 12mm conventional laparoscopic trocar on the patient's left side.
The robotic surgical system is docked between the patient's legs, with the surgical assistant positioned on the patient's left side.
Robotic arms must be checked in order to avoid knocking of the arms each other and limiting the range of motion.
The surgeon begins dissection on the right lateral peritoneum at the level of the sacral promontory, using robotic assistance to carefully separate the tissue.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum along the length of the pelvis using robotic assistance, revealing underlying structures within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon continues the dissection into the rectovaginal septum, using robotic instruments to carefully separate the tissue layers in this area.
A vaginal retractor is inserted into the vagina to enhance exposure of the surgical field.

The surgeon employs both sharp and blunt dissection techniques to carefully separate tissue layers within the pelvic region, continuing the dissection initiated on the right lateral peritoneum at the level of the sacral promontory.
The surgeon exposes the levator ani muscles at the upper part of the anal canal after completing dissection around the distal part of the rectum on both sides.
The surgeon estimates the distance to be two to three centimeters above the dentate line, confirmed visually during a digital rectal examination.
The surgeon continues dissection of the rectovaginal septum, employing traction and countertraction techniques to effectively separate the tissue layers.
The surgeon leaves fatty tissue on the posterior surface of the vagina, creating an additional protective layer to prevent mesh erosion during the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon avoids dissecting the lateral stalks of the rectum and the posterior surface of the mesorectum, maintaining the integrity of these structures during the procedure.
The rectum is positioned within the presacral space, indicating its placement after dissection.
The surgeon positions a non-resorbable L-shaped Marcelin mesh over the rectum for rectopexy, ensuring it is securely placed within the presacral space.
The surgeon cuts the non-resorbable L-shaped Marcelin mesh, preparing it for placement during the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon applies and spreads the non-resorbable L-shaped Marcelin mesh anteriorly over the rectum, with the long limb positioned on the right side.
The surgeon positions the mesh as low as possible between the rectum and vagina, ensuring it is interposed to support the anatomical structures during the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon carefully sutures the lower end of the mesh to the levator muscles on either side of the rectum, securing the mesh in place for the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon positions the non-resorbable L-shaped Marcelin mesh adjacent to the rectum, ensuring it does not directly contact the rectal tissue to prevent mesh erosion.
The surgeon dissects an avascular area over the promontory and anterior prevertebral ligament, preparing the site for mesh attachment.
The surgeon fixes the mesh to the sacral promontory using one or two stitches, ensuring there is no tension on the mesh.
The surgeon places an additional suture to secure the upper part of the vagina to the mesh, effectively obliterating the Douglas pouch.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum over the mesh, covering the previously secured structures and completing the rectopexy procedure.
At first post-operative day, oral intake is started and bladder catheter is removed and patients are usually discharged post-operative day three.
In our approach of rectopexy, important surgical steps have been facilitated by the robotic assistants.
The surgeon performs a complete dissection of the rectovaginal septum, carefully separating the tissue layers to expose the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a gentle dissection along the lateral stults of the rectum and the posterior rectal wall, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon sutures the mesh to the levator muscles on both sides of the anal canal, securing the mesh in place as part of the rectopexy procedure.
